questions.question: What is the percentage of zinc oxide in an ointment prepared by mixing 100 g of 10% ointment, 150 g of 30% ointment, and 60 g of 5% ointment? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume/weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 10% = 0.1, 30% = 0.3, 5% = 0.05
100 g × 0.1 = 10 g (zinc oxide)
150 g × 0.3 = 45 g (zinc oxide)
60 g × 0.05 = 3 g (zinc oxide)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 10 g + 45 g + 3 g = 58 g (zinc oxide)

steps.text: Add all the volumes or weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 100 g + 150 g + 60 g = 310 g (total quantity of all components)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to % if needed
steps.hint:
58 g zinc oxide/310 g ointment = X g zinc oxide/100 g ointment
X = 58 g zinc oxide/310 g ointment × 100 g ointment = 18.7%

questions.question: How many milliliters of 25% w/v dextrose solution and how many milliliters of 15% w/v dextrose solution are required to prepare 5000 ml of a 20% w/v solution?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 25% w/v dextrose solution, the lower concentration is 15% w/v dextrose solution, and the desired concentration is 20% w/v dextrose solution

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
25% - 20% = 5 parts of 15% solution
20% - 15% = 5 parts of 25% solution
Total parts = 10

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For 25%: 5 parts/10 parts = X ml/5000 ml
X = 5 parts/10 parts × 5000 ml = 2500 ml
For 15%: 5 parts/10 parts = X ml/5000 ml
X = 5 parts/10 parts × 5000 ml = 2500 ml

questions.question: What is the percentage of bacitracin in an ointment prepared by mixing 50 g of 25% ointment, 100 g of 15% ointment, and 15 g of 5% ointment? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume/weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 25% = 0.25, 15% = 0.15, 5% = 0.05
50 g × 0.25 = 12.5 g (bacitracin)
100 g × 0.15 = 15 g (bacitracin)
15 g × 0.05 = 0.75 g (bacitracin)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 12.5 g + 15 g + 0.75 g = 28.25 g (bacitracin)

steps.text: Add all the volumes or weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 50 g + 100 g + 15 g = 165 g (total quantity of all components)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to % if needed
steps.hint:
28.25 g bacitracin/165 g ointment = X g bacitracin/100 g ointment
X = 28.25 g bacitracin/165 g ointment × 100 g ointment = 17.12% (rounded to 17.1%)

questions.question: What is the percentage strength of alcohol in a mixture of 250 ml of a solution containing 25% v/v alcohol, 100 ml of a second solution containing 40% v/v alcohol, and a significant quantity of nonalcoholic third solution to make a total of 500 ml? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume by its concentration to calculate the amount of alcohol
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 25% = 0.25, 40% = 0.40
250 ml × 0.25 = 62.5 ml (alcohol)
100 ml × 0.40 = 40 ml (alcohol)
Third solution contains 0% alcohol

steps.text: Note the total volume of the mixture
steps.hint: As given in the question, the total volume is 500 ml

steps.text: Add all the alcohol content to get the total alcohol volume
steps.hint: 62.5 ml + 40 ml + 0 ml = 102.5 ml (total alcohol)

steps.text: Divide total alcohol content by total volume, then convert to %
steps.hint:
102.5 ml alcohol/500 ml solution = X ml alcohol/100 ml solution
X = 102.5 ml alcohol/500 ml solution × 100 ml solution = 20.5%

questions.question: What is the percentage strength of alcohol in a tincture of 450 ml of a solution containing 15% v/v alcohol, 200 ml of a second solution containing 30% v/v alcohol, and a significant quantity of nonalcoholic third solution to make a total of 1000 ml?.

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume by its concentration to calculate the amount of alcohol
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 15% = 0.15, 30% = 0.30
450 ml × 0.15 = 67.5 ml (alcohol)
200 ml × 0.30 = 60 ml (alcohol)
Third solution contains 0% alcohol

steps.text: Note the total volume of the mixture
steps.hint: As given in the question, the total volume is 1000 ml

steps.text: Add all the alcohol content to get the total alcohol volume
steps.hint: 67.5 ml + 60 ml + 0 ml = 127.5 ml (total alcohol)

steps.text: Divide total alcohol content by total volume, then convert to %
steps.hint:
127.5 ml alcohol/1000 ml solution = X ml alcohol/100 ml solution
X = 127.5 ml alcohol/1000 ml solution × 100 ml solution = 12.75%

questions.question: You need to make a 75% alcohol preparation. You only have 95% alcohol and 50% alcohol in stock. What proportions of each strength do you need?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 95% alcohol, the lower concentration is 50% alcohol, and the desired concentration is 75% alcohol

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
95% - 75% = 20 parts of 50% solution
75% - 50% = 25 parts of 95% solution
Total parts = 45

steps.text: Express the result as a ratio of parts
steps.hint: The ratio is 25 parts of 95% alcohol to 20 parts of 50% alcohol

questions.question: How many milliliters each of a 7% solution w/v and 15% w/v solution should be used to prepare a gallon of a 10% w/v solution?

steps.text: Convert all units to standard units (i.e., gallons to mL)
steps.hint: 1 gallon = 3,785 mL (rounded to nearest mL)

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 15% w/v solution, the lower concentration is 7% w/v solution, and the desired concentration is 10% w/v solution

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
15% - 10% = 5 parts of 7% solution
10% - 7% = 3 parts of 15% solution
Total parts = 8

steps.text: Express the result as a ratio of parts
steps.hint: The ratio is 3 parts of 15% solution to 5 parts of 7% solution

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For 15%: 3 parts/8 parts = X mL/3785 mL
X = 3 parts/8 parts × 3785 mL = 1419.375 mL (rounded to 1419 mL)
For 7%: 5 parts/8 parts = X mL/3785 mL
X = 5 parts/8 parts × 3785 mL = 2365.625 mL (rounded to 2366 mL)

questions.question: You combined the contents of a 60 g tube of a 10% ointment and a 100 g tube of a 20% ointment. What is the concentration of the mixture?

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 10% = 0.10, 20% = 0.20
60 g × 0.10 = 6 g (active ingredient)
100 g × 0.20 = 20 g (active ingredient)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 6 g + 20 g = 26 g (total active ingredients)

steps.text: Add all the weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 60 g + 100 g = 160 g (total quantity)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to %
steps.hint:
26 g active/160 g mixture = X g active/100 g mixture
X = 26 g active/160 g mixture × 100 g mixture = 16.25%

questions.question: A pharmacist combined the contents of a 150 g tube of a 66% ointment and a 50 g tube of a 41% ointment. What is the concentration of the mixture?

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 66% = 0.66, 41% = 0.41
150 g × 0.66 = 99 g (active ingredient)
50 g × 0.41 = 20.5 g (active ingredient)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 99 g + 20.5 g = 119.5 g (total active ingredients)

steps.text: Add all the weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 150 g + 50 g = 200 g (total quantity)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to %
steps.hint:
119.5 g active/200 g mixture = X g active/100 g mixture
X = 119.5 g active/200 g mixture × 100 g mixture = 59.75%

questions.question: How many grams of 6% clotrimazole cream should be mixed with 4.5 g of 17% cream to make a 13% clotrimazole cream? Round final answer to the nearest hundredth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 17% cream, the lower concentration is 6% cream, and the desired concentration is 13% cream

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
17% - 13% = 4 parts of 6% cream
13% - 6% = 7 parts of 17% cream
Total parts = 11

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions
steps.hint:
4 parts 6%/7 parts 17% = X g 6%/4.5 g 17%
X = 4 parts × 4.5 g/7 parts = 2.57 g of 6% cream needed

steps.text: Solve for X

questions.question: How many milligrams of 12% hydrocortisone cream should be mixed with 160 mg of 20% cream to make a 15% hydrocortisone cream? Round final answer to the nearest whole number.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 20% cream, the lower concentration is 12% cream, and the desired concentration is 15% cream

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
20% - 15% = 5 parts of 12% cream
15% - 12% = 3 parts of 20% cream
Total parts = 8

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions
steps.hint: 5 parts 12%/3 parts 20% = X mg 12%/160 mg 20%

steps.text: Solve for X
steps.hint: X = 5 parts × 160 mg/3 parts = 266.67 mg, rounded to 267 mg of 12% cream needed

questions.question: If a pharmacist strengthens 20 g of a 0.5% w/w tacrolimus ointment by adding 15 g of an ointment containing 0.06% w/w of the same drug, what would be the percentage strength of the mixture? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 0.5% = 0.005, 0.06% = 0.0006
20 g × 0.005 = 0.1 g (active ingredient)
15 g × 0.0006 = 0.009 g (active ingredient)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 0.1 g + 0.009 g = 0.109 g (total active ingredients)

steps.text: Add all the weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 20 g + 15 g = 35 g (total quantity)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to %
steps.hint:
0.109 g active/35 g mixture = X g active/100 g mixture
X = 0.109 g active/35 g mixture × 100 g mixture = 0.31142857...% (rounded to 0.3%)

questions.question: How many milligrams of zinc oxide should be added to 4500 mg of 8% zinc oxide ointment to prepare an ointment containing 15% zinc oxide?  Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 100% zinc oxide, the lower concentration is 8% zinc oxide, and the desired concentration is 15% zinc oxide

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
100% - 15% = 85 parts of 8% zinc oxide
15% - 8% = 7 parts of 100% zinc oxide
Total parts = 92

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions
steps.hint: 7 parts 100%/85 parts 8% = X mg 100%/4500 mg 8%

steps.text: Solve for X
steps.hint: X = 7 parts × 4500 mg/85 parts = 370.5882352941176 mg, rounded to 370.6 mg of 100% zinc oxide needed

questions.question: How many microliters of acetic acid would need to be added to 2500 mcl of a 15% acetic acid solution to change its concentration to 18%? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 100% acetic acid, the lower concentration is 15% acetic acid, and the desired concentration is 18% acetic acid

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
100% - 18% = 82 parts of 15% acetic acid
18% - 15% = 3 parts of 100% acetic acid
Total parts = 85

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions
steps.hint: 3 parts 100%/82 parts 15% = X mcl 100%/2500 mcl 15%

steps.text: Solve for X
steps.hint: X = 3 parts × 2500 mcl/82 parts = 91.4634146341463 mcl, rounded to 91.5 mcl of 100% acetic acid needed

questions.question: If a pharmacist strengthens 5 g of a 22% w/w ketoconazole ointment by adding 18 g of an ointment containing 15% w/w of the same drug, what would be the percentage strength of the mixture? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 22% = 0.22, 15% = 0.15
5 g × 0.22 = 1.1 g (ketoconazole)
18 g × 0.15 = 2.7 g (ketoconazole)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 1.1 g + 2.7 g = 3.8 g (total ketoconazole)

steps.text: Add all the weights to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 5 g + 18 g = 23 g (total quantity)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to %
steps.hint:
3.8 g active/23 g mixture = X g active/100 g mixture
X = 3.8 g active/23 g mixture × 100 g mixture = 16.5217391304348% (rounded to 16.5%)

questions.question: You receive a prescription for 60 g of a 2% w/w voltaren gel. How many grams each of a 5% w/w voltaren gel and gel base must be used to fill this prescription?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 5% voltaren gel, the lower concentration is 0% (gel base), and the desired concentration is 2% voltaren gel

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
5% - 2% = 3 parts of 0% gel base
2% - 0% = 2 parts of 5% voltaren gel
Total parts = 5

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions for 5% voltaren gel
steps.hint:
2 parts/5 total parts = X g/60 g total
X = 2 parts × 60 g ÷ 5 parts = 24 g of 5% voltaren gel needed

steps.text: Set up the ratios and proportions for gel base
steps.hint:
3 parts/5 total parts = X g/60 g total
X = 3 parts × 60 g ÷ 5 parts = 36 g of gel base needed

questions.question: If equal portions of carbomer gel, 0.5% w/w and .06% w/w, are combined, what would be the resulting percentage strength?

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentage to decimal: 0.5% = 0.005, 0.06% = 0.0006
100 parts × 0.005 = 0.5 parts (carbomer)
100 parts × 0.0006 = 0.06 parts (carbomer)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content
steps.hint: 0.5 parts + 0.06 parts = 0.56 parts (total carbomer)

steps.text: Add all the portions to get the total quantity of the mixture
steps.hint: 100 parts + 100 parts = 200 parts (total quantity)

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to %
steps.hint:
0.56 parts active/200 parts total = X parts active/100 parts total
X = 0.56/2 = 0.28%

questions.question: How many grams of ointment base must be added to 30 g of clobetasol ointment, 5% w/w, to change its strength to 2% w/w?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 5% clobetasol ointment, the lower concentration is 0% (ointment base), and the desired concentration is 2% clobetasol ointment

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
5% - 2% = 3 parts of 0% base
2% - 0% = 2 parts of 5% ointment
Total parts = 5

steps.text: Set up proportion to find amount of base needed
steps.hint: If 2 parts = 30 g of 5% ointment, then X/30 = 3/2 to find grams of base needed

steps.text: Solve for X
steps.hint: Amount of base = 30 g × (3 parts/2 parts) = 45 g of ointment base needed

questions.question:
Retinol Palmitate Ointment 1% 5 g    
Sig. Apply to the eye
How many grams of 5% ophthalmic retinol palmitate ointment and how many grams of ophthalmic base should be used to prepare this prescription?
answerUnit: g of 5% ophthalmic retinol palmitate ointment

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 5% retinol palmitate ointment, the lower concentration is 0% (ophthalmic base), and the desired concentration is 1% retinol palmitate ointment

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
5% - 1% = 4 parts of 0% base
1% - 0% = 1 part of 5% ointment
Total parts = 5

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
1 part/5 parts = X g/5 g
X = 1 part × 5 g/5 parts = 1 g of 5% ointment needed

steps.text: Solve for X
steps.hint: 1 g of 5% ointment is needed

steps.text: Calculate the amount of ophthalmic base needed
steps.hint: Amount of base = Total amount - Amount of 5% ointment = 5 g - 1 g = 4 g

questions.question:
Cyclosporine          5%
Corn Oil              qs
Sig. Use as directed.

In what proportion should corn oil be mixed with a 15% solution of cyclosporine to prepare the prescription?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 15% cyclosporine solution, the lower concentration is 0% (corn oil), and the desired concentration is 5% cyclosporine

steps.text: Set up the allegation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
15% - 5% = 10 parts of 0% corn oil
5% - 0% = 5 parts of 15% solution
Simplify ratio 10:5 to 2:1

steps.text: Express the final ratio
steps.hint: The ratio should be expressed as 2 parts of 15% cyclosporine solution : 1 part of corn oil

questions.question: If equal portions of clotrimazole gel (0.04% w/w, 0.25% w/w, and 1% w/w) are combined, what would be the resulting percentage strength?

steps.text: Multiply each component's weight by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentages to decimals: 0.04% = 0.0004, 0.25% = 0.0025, 1% = 0.01
100 parts × 0.0004 = 0.04 parts (clotrimazole)
100 parts × 0.0025 = 0.25 parts (clotrimazole)
100 parts × 0.01 = 1.0 parts (clotrimazole)

steps.text: Add the amounts of active ingredients
steps.hint: 0.04 parts + 0.25 parts + 1.0 parts = 1.29 parts total clotrimazole

steps.text: Calculate the total volume and divide the total active ingredient by it to get the final concentration
steps.hint:
Total volume = 3 × 100 parts = 300 parts (100 parts from each portion)
Final concentration = (1.29 parts/300 parts) × 100 = 0.43%

questions.question: A pharmacist combines 4 fluid ounces of a povidone-iodine cleaner, 10% w/v, with 6 fluid ounces of a povidone-iodine topical solution, 18% w/v. What is the resulting strength of the povidone-iodine mixture? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Convert all units to standard units (i.e., fluid ounces to mL)
steps.hint:
Convert fluid ounces to milliliters: 1 fl oz = 30 mL
4 fl oz × 30 mL/fl oz = 120 mL
6 fl oz × 30 mL/fl oz = 180 mL

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume by its concentration to calculate the amount of povidone-iodine
steps.hint:
Convert percentages to decimals: 10% = 0.10, 18% = 0.18
120 mL × 0.10 = 12 g povidone-iodine
180 mL × 0.18 = 32.4 g povidone-iodine

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content and add all volumes to or weights to get the total volume or weight
steps.hint:
Total povidone-iodine = 12 g + 32.4 g = 44.4 g
Total volume = 120 mL + 180 mL = 300 mL

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to % if needed
steps.hint:
44.4 g povidone-iodine/300 mL solution = X g povidone-iodine/100 mL solution
X = 44.4 g povidone-iodine/300 mL solution × 100 mL solution = 14.8%

questions.question: A technician combined 1250 ml of a NaCl solution, 0.9% w/v, with 500 ml of a NaCl solution, 0.45% w/v. What is the resulting strength of the NaCl solution mixture?

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentages to decimals: 0.9% = 0.009, 0.45% = 0.0045
1250 mL × 0.009 = 11.25 g NaCl
500 mL × 0.0045 = 2.25 g NaCl

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total active content and add all volumes to or weights to get the total volume or weight
steps.hint:
Total NaCl = 11.25 g + 2.25 g = 13.5 g
Total volume = 1250 mL + 500 mL = 1750 mL

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to % if needed
steps.hint:
13.5 g NaCl/1750 mL solution = X g NaCl/100 mL solution
X = 13.5 g NaCl/1750 mL solution × 100 mL solution = 0.77142...% ≈ 0.77%

questions.question:
Cyclosporine 5%
Corn Oil qs
Sig. Use as directed.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 15% cyclosporine solution, the lower concentration is 0% (corn oil), and the desired concentration is 5% cyclosporine

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component

15% - 5% = 10 parts of 0% (corn oil)
5% - 0% = 5 parts of 15% solution
Total parts = 15

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion

X = 10 parts/15 parts × 50 ml = 33.3 ml
For 15% solution: 5 parts/15 parts = X ml/50 ml
X = 5 parts/15 parts × 50 ml = 16.7 ml

questions.question: How many milliliters of a 4% infliximab injection and how many milliliters of normal saline injection should be used to prepare 250 ml of a 2% infliximab injection? Round final answers to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 4% infliximab injection, the lower concentration is 0% (normal saline), and the desired concentration is 2% infliximab

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
4% - 2% = 2 parts of 0% (normal saline)
2% - 0% = 2 parts of 4% injection
Total parts = 4

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For normal saline: 2 parts/4 parts = X ml/250 ml
X = 2 parts/4 parts × 250 ml = 125 ml
For 4% injection: 2 parts/4 parts = X ml/250 ml
X = 2 parts/4 parts × 250 ml = 125 ml

questions.question:
Mometasone Furoate Cream 0.1% 15 g
Sig. Apply to the affected area four times a day.
How many grams of 5% mometasone furoate cream and how many grams of cream base should be used to prepare this prescription? Round final answers to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 5% mometasone furoate cream, the lower concentration is 0% (cream base), and the desired concentration is 0.1% mometasone furoate

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
5% - 0.1% = 4.9 parts of 0% (cream base)
0.1% - 0% = 0.1 parts of 5% cream
Total parts = 5

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For cream base: 4.9 parts/5 parts = X g/15 g
X = 4.9 parts/5 parts × 15 g = 14.7 g
For 5% cream: 0.1 parts/5 parts = X g/15 g
X = 0.1 parts/5 parts × 15 g = 0.3 g

questions.question: What is the percentage strength of dextrose in a mixture of 450 ml of a solution containing 5% w/v dextrose, 100 ml of a second solution containing 15% w/v dextrose, and a third solution containing 500 ml of 0.9% w/v NaCl? Round final answer to the nearest hundredth.

steps.text: Multiply each component's volume by its concentration to calculate the amount of active ingredient
steps.hint:
Convert percentages to decimals: 5% = 0.05, 15% = 0.15
450 mL × 0.05 = 22.5 g dextrose
100 mL × 0.15 = 15 g dextrose
500 mL × 0 = 0 g dextrose (NaCl solution contains no dextrose)

steps.text: Add all the active ingredients to get the total dextrose content
steps.hint: Total dextrose = 22.5 g + 15 g = 37.5 g

steps.text: Calculate the total volume of the mixture
steps.hint: Total volume = 450 mL + 100 mL + 500 mL = 1050 mL

steps.text: Divide total active content by total quantity, then convert to % if needed
steps.hint:
37.5 g dextrose/1050 mL solution = X g dextrose/100 mL solution
X = 37.5 g dextrose/1050 mL solution × 100 mL solution = 3.57%

questions.question: How many grams of ointment base must be added to 1525 mg of mupirocin ointment, 9% w/w, to change its strength to 1% w/w? Round final answer to the nearest hundredth.

steps.text: Convert all units to standard units (i.e., mg to g)
steps.hint: Convert milligrams to grams by dividing by 1000: 1525 mg = 1.525 g

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 9% mupirocin ointment, the lower concentration is 0% (ointment base), and the desired concentration is 1% mupirocin

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
9% - 1% = 8 parts of 0% (ointment base)
1% - 0% = 1 part of 9% ointment
Total parts = 9

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For ointment base: 1 part/8 parts = 1.525 g/X g
X = 1.525 g × 8/1 = 12.2 g

questions.question: You receive a prescription for 100 ml of a 3.5% lidocaine hydrochloride ophthalmic gel. How many milliliters each of a 12% and 1% lidocaine hydrochloride ophthalmic gel must be used? Round final answers to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 12% lidocaine gel, the lower concentration is 1% lidocaine gel, and the desired concentration is 3.5% lidocaine gel

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
12% - 3.5% = 8.5 parts of 1% gel
3.5% - 1% = 2.5 parts of 12% gel
Total parts = 11

steps.text: Convert parts to actual quantity using proportion
steps.hint:
For 12% gel: 2.5 parts/11 parts = X ml/100 ml
X = 2.5 parts/11 parts × 100 ml = 22.7 ml
For 1% gel: 8.5 parts/11 parts = X ml/100 ml
X = 8.5 parts/11 parts × 100 ml = 77.3 ml

questions.question: If 30 g of a combination cream of 5% w/w neomycin sulfate and 10% w/w polymyxin B sulfate are mixed with 10 g of a cream containing 9% w/w neomycin sulfate and 15% w/w polymyxin B, calculate the percentage strength of each of the two drugs in the mixture.

steps.text: Calculate the total weight of the mixture
steps.hint: Total weight = 30 g + 10 g = 40 g

steps.text: Part 1: Calculate the amount of neomycin sulfate in each cream and total amount
steps.hint:
First cream: 30 g × 5% = 1.5 g neomycin
Second cream: 10 g × 9% = 0.9 g neomycin
Total neomycin = 1.5 g + 0.9 g = 2.4 g

steps.text: Calculate the final percentage of neomycin sulfate
steps.hint: Neomycin percentage = (2.4 g ÷ 40 g) × 100 = 6%

steps.text: Part 2: Calculate the amount of polymyxin B in each cream and total amount
steps.hint:
First cream: 30 g × 10% = 3 g polymyxin
Second cream: 10 g × 15% = 1.5 g polymyxin
Total polymyxin = 3 g + 1.5 g = 4.5 g

steps.text: Calculate the final percentage of polymyxin B
steps.hint: Polymyxin percentage = (4.5 g ÷ 40 g) × 100 = 11.25%

questions.question: In what proportion should 0.5% ketoconazole cream be mixed with a 3% ketoconazole cream to produce a 2% ketoconazole cream?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 3% ketoconazole cream, the lower concentration is 0.5% ketoconazole cream, and the desired concentration is 2% ketoconazole cream

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
3% - 2% = 1 part of 0.5% cream
2% - 0.5% = 1.5 parts of 3% cream
Total parts = 2.5

steps.text: Find the proportion
steps.hint:
1 part of 0.5% cream
1.5 parts of 3% cream
Final ratio is 1:1.5

questions.question: If 120 ml of a combination gel of hydrocortisone acetate 5% w/w, and pramoxine 2% w/w, are mixed with 60 ml of a gel containing hydrocortisone acetate 1% w/w, and pramoxine 2% w/w, calculate the percentage strength of each of the two drugs in the mixture. Round final answer to nearest tenth.

steps.text: Calculate the total volume of the mixture
steps.hint: Total volume = 120 ml + 60 ml = 180 ml

steps.text: Part 1: Calculate the amount of hydrocortisone acetate in each gel and total amount
steps.hint:
First gel: 120 ml × 5% = 6 ml hydrocortisone
Second gel: 60 ml × 1% = 0.6 ml hydrocortisone
Total hydrocortisone = 6 ml + 0.6 ml = 6.6 ml

steps.text: Calculate the final percentage of hydrocortisone acetate
steps.hint: Hydrocortisone percentage = (6.6 ml ÷ 180 ml) × 100 = 3.7%

steps.text: Part 2: Calculate the amount of pramoxine in each gel and total amount
steps.hint:
First gel: 120 ml × 2% = 2.4 ml pramoxine
Second gel: 60 ml × 2% = 1.2 ml pramoxine
Total pramoxine = 2.4 ml + 1.2 ml = 3.6 ml

steps.text: Calculate the final percentage of pramoxine
steps.hint: Pramoxine percentage = (3.6 ml ÷ 180 ml) × 100 = 2.0%

questions.question: How many milliliters of 0.45% NaCl and how many milliliters of 2% NaCl are needed to make 1 pint of normal saline solution? Round final answers to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Convert 1 pint to milliliters
steps.hint: 1 pint = 473 mL

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 2% NaCl, the lower concentration is 0.45% NaCl, and normal saline is 0.9% NaCl

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
2% - 0.9% = 1.1 parts of 0.45% NaCl
0.9% - 0.45% = 0.45 parts of 2% NaCl
Total parts = 1.55

steps.text: Convert parts to actual volumes using proportion
steps.hint:
For 0.45% NaCl: 1.1 parts/1.55 parts = X ml/473 ml
X = 1.1/1.55 × 473 = 335.7 ml
For 2% NaCl: 0.45 parts/1.55 parts = X ml/473 ml
X = 0.45/1.55 × 473 = 137.3 ml

questions.question: In what proportion should betamethasone dipropionate be added to betamethasone dipropionate lotion 1% to make a 5% lotion?

steps.text: Identify known concentrations (one higher, one lower, and one middle/desired)
steps.hint: Based on the question, the higher concentration is 100% (pure betamethasone dipropionate), the lower concentration is 1% lotion, and the desired concentration is 5% lotion

steps.text: Set up the alligation scheme (alligation tic-tac-toe), plug in known values, and calculate parts of each component
steps.hint:
100% - 5% = 95 parts of 1% lotion
5% - 1% = 4 parts of 100% betamethasone
Total parts = 99

steps.text: Find the proportion
steps.hint:
4 parts of 100% betamethasone
95 parts of 1% lotion
Final ratio is 4:95
